retrieved_docs.title: Female Infertility -- Pathophysiology -- Endometriosis
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is defined as endometrial tissue outside the uterine cavity. The diagnosis is based on the histological identification of endometrial glands or stroma outside the uterus. Endometriosis is most commonly found in the pelvis but can spread throughout the entire abdomen and affects 10% to 15% of reproductive-age women. [19] Of women with endometriosis, 40% to 50% experience infertility. [20] Endometriosis is categorized into 4 stages, according to the American Society of Reproductive Medicine, with stage I being minimal and stage IV severe. Endometriosis is known to cause infertility, but the pathophysiology is thought to change according to the stage. [21] For stages I and II, infertility is believed to be associated with inflammation and increased production of prostaglandins and cytokines, macrophages, and natural killer cells. [22] The inflammation impairs ovarian and tubal function, resulting in defective follicular formation, fertilization, and implantation. [23] Stages III and IV are associated with pelvic adhesions or masses that distort pelvic anatomy; this inherently impairs tubal motility, oocyte release, and sperm motility. [24] Also, advanced endometriosis is hypothesized to impair folliculogenesis, reducing fertilization potential. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The main complications of endometriosis include infertility or subfertility, chronic pain, and other debilitating persistent symptoms. These are wide in range, including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and dyschezia. Still, endometriosis can also cause a decrease in the quality of life of patients, complications of surgical procedures, anatomical abnormalities due to possible adhesions, bowel or/and bladder dysfunction, but in the case of ovarian endometriomas could even lead to the development of cancer. The role of endometriosis in infertile women has been debated for a long time, with literature research showing that infertile women are 6 up to 8-fold more likely to suffer from endometriosis than fertile females. [42] Even though several mechanisms have been proposed to explain this phenomenon, no consensus has been reached in the scientific community about the exact mechanism behind infertility in endometriosis. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: In Vitro Fertilization -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory disease defined by the presence of endometrial tissue outside of the uterine cavity, is significantly more prevalent in women with infertility compared to those without. [7] The mechanisms of how endometriosis causes infertility are not yet fully understood, but research has shown that pelvic adhesions, chronic intraperitoneal inflammation, disturbed folliculogenesis, and decreased embryo implantation have all been described in women with endometriosis. Laparoscopic surgery has been found to increase the rate of pregnancy from 4.7% to 30.7%, indicating the importance of restoring normal pelvic anatomy for a spontaneous pregnancy. Unfortunately, women with endometriosis have a significantly lower success rate with IVF compared to other causes of infertility, with more advanced disease correlated to inferior outcomes. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Female Infertility -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The World Health Organization (WHO) performed a large multinational study to determine gender distribution and infertility etiologies. In 37% of infertile couples, female infertility was the cause; in 35% of couples, both male and female causes were identified; in 8%, there was male factor infertility. [3] In the same study, the most common identifiable factors of female infertility are as follows: Ovulatory disorders: 25% Endometriosis: 15% Pelvic adhesions: 12% Tubal blockage: 11% Other tubal/uterine abnormalities: 11% Hyperprolactinemia: 7%

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrioma -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cytokines and prostaglandins are signaling molecules for creating an inflammatory response and thus generate inflammation in the area of the endometriotic implantation. This inflammatory response then lays the foundation for the production of new vascularization and new fibrous tissue formation. This snowball effect then creates the adhesions and pain commonly associated with this disease process. These issues also lead to the main complications of this disease, such as infertility and chronic pelvic pain. Patients with endometriomas have a more severe disease state and typically experience this on a more significant scale than those with stage one or two endometriosis. [9] [25] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Female Reproduction -- Pathophysiology -- Infertility
retrieved_docs.content: Fecundability is the ability to conceive. A successful pregnancy requires a complex sequence of events, including ovulation, fertilization, and implantation into a viable uterine cavity. The probability of conceiving during timed intercourse (ovulation) is approximately 16% to 28%, though percentages can vary slightly between studies. [22] Infertility is defined as the failure to achieve pregnancy after 1 year (12 months) of regular, unprotected sexual intercourse. [23] Primary infertility is designated to individuals who have never conceived, while secondary infertility applies to those who have conceived in the past but are now experiencing infertility. Either female factors, male factors, or both can cause infertility. The most common female factors leading to infertility, in decreasing order, include ovulatory disorders, endometriosis, pelvic adhesions, tubal blockage, other uterine/tubal abnormalities, and hyperprolactinemia. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrioma -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis, in general, has been found to affect approximately 10% of women of reproductive age. However, only about 3% of reproductive-aged women have clinically significant disease. [9] Among this 3%, there are specific populations in which endometriosis is quite prevalent. For example, endometriosis has been found in nearly 50% of women experiencing issues with infertility and nearly 70% of women with pelvic pain. [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrioma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is a common, estrogen-dependent, inflammatory, gynecologic disease process in which normal endometrial tissue is abnormally present outside the uterine cavity. Endometriomas are cystic lesions that stem from endometriosis. Endometriomas are most commonly found in the ovaries. Endometriosis affects approximately 10% of reproductive-aged women and is a common cause of chronic pain, dyspareunia, dysmenorrhea, and infertility. Most commonly, endometriosis is found within the pelvis, specifically on the ovaries. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis remains a disease with high comorbidity, even though extensive research has been conducted and new medical treatments have been developed. Healthcare professionals need to join hands and work collectively when they need to work with patients with suspected cases of endometriosis. It is not random that the mean delay of a definitive diagnosis of endometriosis is approximately 10 years. Public health interventions should be introduced and promote awareness among females of childbearing age for signs and symptoms of endometriosis; thus, they could seek medical care in an earlier stage of the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Uterus -- Pathophysiology -- Endometriosis
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is a chronic and painful gynecologic disease that typically presents with pelvic pain, AUB, dysmenorrhea, and/or dyspareunia. Dyschezia (pain with defecation) can occur if endometrial tissue is implanted in the pouch of Douglas. If the intestines or bladder are involved, abdominal pain and painful urination will occur, respectively. Symptoms typically vary with hormone changes and often get worse during menstruation. The inflammation associated with endometriosis has the potential to damage or scar the fallopian tubes and ovaries, leading to infertility. On physical exam, the uterus will be normal in size.

retrieved_docs.title: Endometritis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the clinical manifestations and risk factors associated with endometriosis, such as dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility. Utilize appropriate diagnostic tools and assessments to screen for endometriosis in symptomatic patients. Tailor treatment strategies to individual patient needs, considering factors like symptom severity, desire for fertility, and comorbid conditions. Coordinate care and follow-up to ensure seamless coordination of care, referrals, and long-term follow-up to optimize patient outcomes and quality of life. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment of endometriosis is broadly categorized into 2 main categories: pharmacological and surgical. Currently, there is no specific drug that could inhibit the progress of the disease other than hormonal and non-hormonal agents used to alleviate the symptoms and increase fertility rates. [32] An empirical medical therapy can be instituted for women with endometriosis symptoms (ie, pelvic pain) even without histological confirmation of the disease [31] . It should be highlighted that since endometriosis is a chronic disease, the treatment is mainly used to alleviate symptoms and not ultimately for the cure. [31] Providers should always remember that response to the therapy does not verify the diagnosis of endometriosis. [31] Many physicians treat women who suffer from persistent pain empirically after taking a detailed history, conducting a thorough physical examination, and excluding other pathologies, even though medical treatment does not improve fertility. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of endometriosis in the majority of women is often delayed. Thus, women unavoidably suffer from the pain and the long-term effects of this debilitating disease, including infertility. In women with infertility, endometriosis is up to 50%, whereas, in adolescents, the incidence of endometriosis is reported to be 47% of those who have experienced laparoscopy for pelvic pain. [15] [14] Several predisposing factors have been linked with the risk of developing endometriosis. Early age at menarche (age below 11 years old), shorter duration of menstrual periods(less than 27 days), heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia), and nulliparity increase the risk for endometriosis, indicating and ascertaining the hypothesis that endometriosis is closely linked with the hormonal status of a woman(high estrogen levels and low progesterone). [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the risk factors for developing endometriosis and summarize the epidemiology of the disease. Evaluate the history and physical exam findings associated with endometriosis and describe the available ways to evaluate the disease. Differentiate the treatment and management options available for endometriosis. Communicate the importance of interprofessional team collaboration to improve the outcomes of endometriosis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrioma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The gold standard for the diagnosis of endometriosis is via laparoscopy. Because imaging and laboratory studies are of limited benefit in diagnosing endometriosis, direct visualization through surgery is the standard. During laparoscopy, endometriosis lesions will typically appear blue or black. However, they can appear as red, white, or non-pigmented lesions. At this time, the severity of the disease can also be evaluated. If there are significant adhesions, peritoneal defects, or endometriomas present, this is indicative of a more severe disease. The visualized lesions can be biopsied and evaluated by pathology for endometrial glands and stroma. [36] If the patient is also experiencing infertility issues, chromotubation can also be completed at this time to assess tubal patency.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2692
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is recognized as an allowable therapeutic option for both female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and uterine endometriosis (انتباذ بطاني رحمي). These two gynecologic conditions are interrelated, as uterine endometriosis is a known contributor to infertility in women. Consequently, radiotherapy may be considered within the broader management strategies for these linked disorders, reflecting their clinical connection and the potential role of radiotherapeutic interventions in addressing both conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2764
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is a gynecological disorder characterized by the presence of endometrial‑like tissue that is normally located in the uterus, and its ectopic growth can cause pain, inflammation, and fertility issues. Understanding that endometriosis originates from tissue associated with the uterus is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2756
retrieved_docs.content: Dietoterapie is recognized as an allowed therapeutic approach for both العقم لدى الإناث (female infertility) and انتباذ بطاني رحمي (endometriosis). These two gynecological conditions are related, suggesting that dietary management may have relevance for the pathophysiology and treatment of each. Consequently, clinicians should consider incorporating diet therapy when addressing either العقم لدى الإناث or انتباذ بطاني رحمي, as dietary interventions could potentially benefit both interlinked conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1684
retrieved_docs.content: Danazol may be used as a therapeutic option for انتباذ بطاني رحمي (endometriosis). Endometriosis is related to العقم لدى الإناث (female infertility), indicating that the condition can contribute to difficulties in conceiving. Consequently, treatment of انتباذ بطاني رحمي with danazol may help alleviate the underlying pathology and potentially improve reproductive outcomes for women experiencing العقم لدى الإناث.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2535
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and endometrial implantation (انتباذ بطاني رحمي) are interconnected medical conditions. Prevention and control measures are applicable to both female infertility and endometrial implantation, and the two conditions are related, indicating that endometrial implantation can contribute to or be associated with female infertility and that managing endometrial pathology is important in the care of women with infertility.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 827
retrieved_docs.content: ulcers. The framework links female infertility of cervical origin to اضطراب في عنق الرحم and to العقم لدى الإناث, and associates female infertility with endometrial implantation, reflecting the contribution of endometriosis to reproductive difficulty. Diabetic neuropathy is highlighted as a key risk factor for lower‑extremity ulcers, underscoring the need for vigilant monitoring in diabetic patients. Overall, the integrated classification supports comprehensive documentation, risk assessment, and coordinated management across these interconnected medical entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and endometriosis (انتباذ بطاني رحمي) are closely interrelated gynecological disorders, with endometriosis recognized as a contributing factor to infertility in women. Rehabilitation (rehabilitace) is considered an appropriate therapeutic qualifier for both conditions, indicating that structured rehabilitation programs can be incorporated into the management of female infertility and endometriosis to improve reproductive outcomes and alleviate disease‑related symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 552
retrieved_docs.content: Infertility in women of cervical origin is classified as a specific type of cervical disorder and is also considered a subset of female infertility. Both cervical disorders and female infertility are targets for prevention and control strategies, as are peptic ulcers and endometriosis. Endometriosis is linked to female infertility, indicating that the presence of endometrial implants can contribute to difficulties conceiving. Pharmacologic management of endometriosis may include danazol, which has the potential to treat this condition. Implementing preventive and control measures for cervical disorders, peptic ulcers, female infertility, and endometriosis is essential for reducing disease burden and improving reproductive health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2855
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is connected to endometriosis through a broader relationship, indicating that endometriosis falls within the scope of disorders affecting the female reproductive system. Both Female Reproductive System Disorder and endometriosis are classified under the parent category اضطراب رحمي, which translates to “uterine disorder.” In this hierarchy, اضطراب رحمي serves as the overarching class, with Female Reproductive System Disorder representing a broader group of conditions and endometriosis representing a specific type of uterine disorder. This structure highlights how endometriosis is a particular manifestation of uterine pathology within the larger context of female reproductive system disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) is closely related to endometrial implantation (انتباذ بطاني رحمي). Abnormalities in the process of endometrial implantation can impair the uterine lining’s ability to support embryo attachment, thereby contributing to infertility in women. Recognizing the connection between these two conditions is important for clinicians when evaluating reproductive health and planning appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 730
retrieved_docs.content: Infertility in women of cervical origin is identified as a specific disorder of the cervix (اضطراب في عنق الرحم) and represents a subtype of female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث). Female infertility, in turn, is closely linked to intrauterine adhesions (انتباذ بطاني رحمي) and may arise as a consequence of inflammation of the uterine lining (الْتِهابُ بِطانَةِ الرَّحِمِ). These interconnections indicate that cervical pathology, uterine lining inflammation, and intrauterine adhesions can all contribute to the development of female infertility, underscoring the importance of evaluating cervical health, endometrial status, and the presence of adhesions when assessing and managing infertility in women.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 477
retrieved_docs.content: اضطراب الأعضاء التناسلية لدى الإناث. Benign vaginal tumor (ورم مهبلي حميد) is another entity classified within Female Reproductive System Disorder. Collectively, these entities—sexual dysfunction, preterm labor, infertility, uterine cancer, endometriosis, and benign vaginal tumor—are interrelated components of the Female Reproductive System Disorder taxonomy, reflected in major regulatory terminologies and linked by clinical relationships that support integrated diagnostic, therapeutic, and regulatory strategies in reproductive medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1571
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier indicates that therapy (terapie) is permissible for both female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and endometriosis (انتباذ بطاني رحمي). In addition, female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) is related to endometriosis (انتباذ بطاني رحمي), highlighting a clinical connection between these two gynecologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2635
retrieved_docs.content: Manual conversion of position is a medical procedure whose site is the uterus, indicating that it is performed on or involves the uterine organ. Endometriosis is a disease that is located in the uterus, reflecting the presence of ectopic endometrial tissue within or on the uterine structure. Both concepts highlight the uterus as a central focus for therapeutic interventions and pathological conditions, underscoring the importance of uterine health in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 376
retrieved_docs.content: Reproductive System Disorder. Endometriosis exhibits a broader relationship with اضطراب الأعضاء التناسلية لدى الإناث and is linked to اضطراب رحمي through an inverse_isa relationship, indicating that uterine disorder is the more general class encompassing endometriosis. Overall, these relationships depict a multi‑layered network in which sexual function disorders, obstetric complications, and gynecologic conditions intersect. Recognizing that Female Reproductive System Disorder resides at the intersection of خلل الوظيفة الجنسية, pregnancy disorders such as اضطراب الحمل and مخاض قبل الأوان, and uterine pathology provides clinicians with a comprehensive framework for diagnosis, interdisciplinary management, and patient counseling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1712
retrieved_docs.content: Infertiliteit bij vrouw van cervicale oorsprong is classified as a specific type of اضطراب في عنق الرحم, indicating that cervical abnormalities can lead to infertility in women. This condition is also considered a subset of العقم لدى الإناث, placing it within the broader category of female infertility. Moreover, العقم لدى الإناث is related to انتباذ بطاني رحمي, reflecting the clinical observation that endometriosis can contribute to or be associated with infertility in women. Understanding that cervical disorders, as exemplified by Infertiliteit bij vrouw van cervicale oorsprong, and endometriosis are both linked to العقم لدى الإناث helps clinicians evaluate underlying causes and tailor appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.
